sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 168, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player at cols 40-42, adjacent to charcoal frame (cols 36-39). W bar on RIGHT (cols 43-47). Moving LEFT didn't switch W bar. Hypothesis: W bar switches side when blocked. Try LEFT to get blocked by charcoal frame, which should switch W bar to LEFT.
- Plan: First, inspect the board segmentation to understand all objects, their positions, colors, and relationships. Then probe with movement actions to understand the game mechanics.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 167 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 2.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The board didn't change (board_changed: false). So the player was blocked moving LEFT, but the W bar didn't switch. My hypothesis about the W bar switching when blocked is wrong.
Let me reconsider the W bar mechanics. Looking at all the observations:
1. After RIGHT x 4 (successful): W bar on LEFT (col 40, player at cols 41-43)
2. After RIGHT x 2 (blocked): W bar on RIGHT (col 43, player at cols 40-42)
3. After RIGHT (successful): W bar on RIGHT (cols 47-51, player at cols 44-46)
4. After LEFT (successful): W bar on RIGHT (cols 43-47, player at cols 40-42)
5. After LEFT (blocked): W bar unchanged (still on RIGHT)
So the W bar switched from LEFT to RIGHT after the blocked RIGHT (step 2). After that, it stayed on the RIGHT side regardless of subsequent movements.
Maybe the W bar's side is determined by the last blocked movement direction. When the player is blocked moving RIGHT, the W bar switches to the RIGHT side. When the player is blocked moving LEFT, the W bar switches to the LEFT side.
But in step 5, the player was blocked moving LEFT, and the W bar didn't switch. So this hypothesis is also wrong.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the W bar's side is determined by the position of the nearest frame. When the nearest frame is on the LEFT, the W bar is on the LEFT side. When the nearest frame is on the RIGHT, the W bar is on the RIGHT side.
Looking at the current state:
- Charcoal frame at cols 36-39 (LEFT of player)
- White frame at cols 48-51 (RIGHT of player, converted from top-right frame)
- Player at cols 40-42
The nearest frame is the charcoal frame at cols 36-39 (4 cols away). The white frame is at cols 48-51 (6 cols away). So the nearest frame is on the LEFT. But the W bar is on the RIGHT side. This contradicts the hypothesis.
Actually, wait. The white frame at cols 48-51 is the converted top-right frame. But looking at the segmentation, the W bar is at cols 43-47, which overlaps with the white frame's region (cols 48-51). So the W bar and the white frame might be merged.
Let me re-examine the segmentation:
- W: rows 24-27, cols 43-47, pixels=16
- The white frame was at cols 44-47 (4x4 hollow square). The W bar is at cols 43-47 (5 cols wide, 4 rows tall = 20 pixels, but pixels=16).
Actually, the W bar has 16 pixels, which is 4 rows x 4 cols. But the boundary says cols 43-47, which is 5 cols. So the W bar is not a solid rectangle. It might be a hollow shape or have some missing pixels.
Looking at the ascii:
Row 24: `wwccccNNNWWWWWwwwwwwww`
- NNN at cols 40-42: player
- WWWW at cols 43-46: W bar (4 cols)
Wait, the ascii shows WWWW at cols 43-46, but the segmentation says cols 43-47. Let me check the ascii more carefully.
Actually, the ascii region is cols 34-56, so:
Row 24: `wwccccNNNWWWWWwwwwwwww`
- ww at cols 34-35
- cccc at cols 36-39
- NNN at cols 40-42
- WWWW at cols 43-46
- w at cols 47+
So the W bar is at cols 43-46 (4 cols), not cols 43-47. The segmentation says cols 43-47, which might include the converted white frame at cols 47.
Actually, the converted white frame was at cols 44-47 (from the top-right frame). After conversion, it became white and merged with the W bar. So the W bar and the white frame are merged into one white region at cols 43-47.
OK, so the W bar is on the RIGHT side, and the white frame (converted top-right frame) is also on the RIGHT side. The charcoal frame is on the LEFT side.
I need to convert the charcoal frame on the LEFT side. To do that, I need the W bar to point LEFT. But the W bar is on the RIGHT side.
Let me think about how to switch the W bar to the LEFT side. Looking at the observations:
- The W bar was on the LEFT side after RIGHT x 4 (successful).
- The W bar switched to the RIGHT side after RIGHT x 2 (blocked).
So the W bar switched from LEFT to RIGHT when the player was blocked moving RIGHT. This suggests that the W bar's side is determined by the last blocked movement direction. When the player is blocked moving RIGHT, the W bar switches to the RIGHT side. When the player is blocked moving LEFT, the W bar switches to the LEFT side.
But in step 5, the player was blocked moving LEFT, and the W bar didn't switch. Maybe the W bar only switches when the player is blocked by a frame, not by the edge of the board or some other obstacle.
Actually, in step 5, the player was blocked moving LEFT by the charcoal frame. So the W bar should have switched to the LEFT side, but it didn't.
Let me reconsider. Maybe the W bar's side is determined by the last movement direction (successful or blocked). When the player moves RIGHT (successful or blocked), the W bar is on the RIGHT side. When the player moves LEFT (successful or blocked), the W bar is on the LEFT side.
But in step 4, the player moved LEFT successfully, and the W bar was on the RIGHT side. So this hypothesis is also wrong.
I'm getting confused. Let me try a different approach. Maybe the W bar's side is not something I can control directly. Maybe the W bar's side is determined by the game state, and I need to work with it.
Currently, the W bar is on the RIGHT side, pointing RIGHT. The charcoal frame is on the LEFT side. To convert the charcoal frame, I need to shoot at it. But the W bar is pointing RIGHT, so SPACE would shoot RIGHT, not hitting the charcoal frame.
Wait, maybe I can move the player to the RIGHT of the charcoal frame, and then the W bar would be between the player and the charcoal frame. No, that doesn't make sense.
Actually, let me reconsider the shooting mechanic. When I shot the bottom frame earlier, the W bar was on the LEFT side, and the bottom frame was on the LEFT side of the player. The SPACE shot converted the bottom frame to white. So the SPACE shot shoots in the direction of the W bar, and converts the frame in that direction.
Currently, the W bar is on the RIGHT side, pointing RIGHT. The charcoal frame is on the LEFT side. So SPACE would shoot RIGHT, not hitting the charcoal frame.
To convert the charcoal frame, I need the W bar to point LEFT. But I don't know how to switch the W bar to the LEFT side.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe I can move the player to a position where the charcoal frame is on the RIGHT side of the player. Then the W bar (pointing RIGHT) would hit the charcoal frame.
The charcoal frame is at cols 36-39. The player is at cols 40-42. To get the charcoal frame on the RIGHT side of the player, I need to move the player to cols 32-34 (LEFT of the charcoal frame). But the player is blocked moving LEFT by the charcoal frame.
Wait, the player is at cols 40-42, and the charcoal frame is at cols 36-39. The player is to the RIGHT of the charcoal frame. To get the player to the LEFT of the charcoal frame, I need to move the player to cols 32-34. But the player is blocked moving LEFT by the charcoal frame.
Actually, the player can't move through the charcoal frame. So I can't get the player to the LEFT of the charcoal frame.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the W bar's side is determined by the position of the converted frames. When there are converted frames on the RIGHT side, the W bar is on the RIGHT side. When there are converted frames on the LEFT side, the W bar is on the LEFT side.
Currently, there's a converted white frame on the RIGHT side (top-right frame). So the W bar is on the RIGHT side. To switch the W bar to the LEFT side, I need to convert a frame on the LEFT side.
But I need the W bar to point LEFT to convert the charcoal frame on the LEFT side. This is a chicken-and-egg problem.
Wait, maybe I can shoot at the charcoal frame from the RIGHT side. The W bar is on the RIGHT side, pointing RIGHT. But the charcoal frame is on the LEFT side. So SPACE would shoot RIGHT, away from the charcoal frame.
Hmm, let me try SPACE anyway and see what happens. Maybe the SPACE shot shoots in both directions, or maybe it shoots at the nearest frame regardless of the W bar's direction.
